invariant_definition: *Advaita* does not mean a generic 'all is one.' It names the denial of a second independent reality, but the force of that denial depends on what kind of difference is being rejected. In Vedānta the term sits inside disputes about *bheda* and *abheda*, cause and effect, the status of the world, and the relation between *brahman*, *jīva*, and *jagat*. Śaṅkara's Advaita makes non-duality numerical and ultimate: only *brahman* is *pāramārthika*, while plurality is *vyāvahārika* and sublatable. Viśiṣṭādvaita preserves non-duality by denying a second substance outside *brahman* while retaining real internal differentiation as *cit* and *acit*. Vallabha's *śuddhādvaita* preserves non-duality while rejecting *māyā* as illusory appearance: the world is a real manifestation of *brahman*. Nimbārka and Caitanya traditions keep non-duality and difference together by treating dependence on *brahman* as irreducible. The term therefore marks a family of claims about how plurality belongs, or fails to belong, within the real; it is not a free-floating synonym for monism.

school_framing.shared_core: *Advaita* in the per-school entry's invariant sense names **a non-duality thesis: the position that the ultimate is without internal differentiation and without a real second**. The classical Advaita-Vedānta of Śaṅkara is the principal home (*Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*; *Upadeśa-Sāhasrī*). Other schools listed in the entry engage *advaita*-language only to reject, qualify, or reinterpret it: the *śabda* is the same but the doctrinal position differs. Methodology AF1: this is a school-position label, not a concept-with-different-aspects.
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Different concepts**: the per-school entries gloss *advaita* as the doctrinal position each school holds (Advaita: *jīva-brahman-aikya*; Viśiṣṭādvaita: *śarīra-śarīrin* unity; Tattva-vāda: *bhedapañcaka*, not unity; etc.). These are competing M-positions, not different aspects of one shared concept. Methodology AF1 applies globally to the term.

per_school.definition: Advaita names the unborn, sleepless, dreamless reality known when beginningless māyā is undone; duality is *māyāmātra*, while *advaita* alone is *paramārtha* [[MK 1.16-18](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.Āgama-prakaraṇa.16-18)], [[MK 3.19-21](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prakaraṇa.19-21)]. Turīya is *prapañcopaśama*, *śānta*, *śiva*, and *advaita*, the self to be known [[MāṇḍU 7](cite://sankara/mandukya-bhasya/upaniṣad.7)]. The later dialectical tradition accordingly begins by establishing *dvaita-mithyātva* as the preliminary to *advaita-siddhi* [[AdvS 1a](cite://madhusudana/advaita-siddhi/Prathama-pariccheda, Section 1a)].

*Advaita-śruti* does not sublate *prapañca-sadbhāva*; *advitīya* excludes only a second equal or superior to Brahman [[Svs 31-35](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/31-35)]. Brahman is Nārāyaṇa qualified by the totality of *cit* and *acit*, and the śrutis proclaim him as the inner self of all beings and the ground of their ordered dependence [[VS §1](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§1)], [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. The term therefore marks the unsurpassed unity of the whole in which differentiated sentient and non-sentient reals abide as inseparable modes of the one Lord [[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)], [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

Here *advaita* is rejected because reality is ordered as *svatantra* and *asvatantra*; only Viṣṇu is independent, while all other reals remain permanently dependent [[TS 1-2](cite://madhva/tattva-sankhyana/1)], [[VTV 3.1-3.2](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.1)]. The appeal to ignorance cannot ground a secondless self, since whether the loss of non-duality stands apart from the self or not, the doctrine fails on both sides and *māyāvāda* is set aside [[Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, advaita-hāni](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/the loss of non-duality stands apart from the self's nature)], [[Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, anatireke](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/it does not stand apart)], [[Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, tan-matam akhilam](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/the whole doctrine is set aside)].

Non-duality here is the real non-separateness of the differentiated manifold from Brahman, since the unmanifest becomes articulated by *nāma-rūpa* without ceasing to be Brahman [[BṛU 1.4.7](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.7)], [[BṛU 1.4.10](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.10)]. Brahman is taught as both formed and formless, and *ahaṃ brahmāsmi* states an *abheda* that remains compatible with genuine differentiation [[BṛU 2.3](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/2.3)], [[BṛU 1.4.10](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.10)]. The soteriology accordingly retains *śravaṇa*, *manana*, *nididhyāsana*, and *upāsanā* as jointly load-bearing disciplines [[BṛU 2.4.5, 1.4.15](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/2.4.5, 1.4.15)].

The *abheda-śāstras* are to be read together with real *bheda*, because the aggregate of *jīvas* stands to Bhagavān through his *svābhāvika* and *acintya-śakti* as rays to the sun [[PS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)], [[PS §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)]. Once Bhagavān is fixed as the locus of *māyā* and the *jīva* as that which is deluded by *māyā*, worship of him alone becomes the operative *abhidheya* [[PS §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)]. Non-duality here thus names the inseparability of *śakti* and *śaktimat*, with Bhagavān as the *parama-svarūpa* of all and *prema* as the final *prayojana* [[PS §42](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42)], [[PS §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

Advaita here is the *abheda* inseparable from *svābhāvika-bheda*: the *jīva* is of the nature of cognition and dependent on Hari, yet atomic and distinct in each body [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)], [[VPS 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42)]. Because every entity is *brahmātmaka* while the threefold order of reality remains scripturally established, non-duality and plurality are both rooted in the nature of the real itself [[DaŚ 7](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/7)], [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)]. Refuge at Kṛṣṇa's feet therefore discloses a unity that preserves the *jīva*'s enduring *aṃśa*-status [[DaŚ 8](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/8)], [[DaŚ 4](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4)].

*Advaita* means that Brahman alone is real, yet the world proceeds as Brahman's own *pariṇāma* without compromising Brahman's *avikṛtatva* [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)]. The school's load-bearing claim is *avikṛta-pariṇāma*: Brahman becomes the effect-form while remaining fully itself, as gold remains unmodified in many ornaments [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)]. Since attainment of Puruṣottama requires Brahma-knowledge together with devotion as assisting fitness, non-duality opens directly into *bhakti* and grace [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

Here `advaita` is refused at the level of the self, since the plurality of puruṣas is established from the ordered distribution of birth, death, and embodiment [[SS 1.149](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.149)], [[SK 18](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/18)]. The `advaita`-śrutis are read as `jāti-parā`, so they do not cancel numerical difference among knowers [[SS 1.154](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.154)]. Sāṃkhya therefore rejects `ātmādvaita`: the marks of the self disclose difference, and if plurality were reduced to `upādhi` duality would simply return; liberation is instead `kaivalya`, the discriminative isolation of puruṣa from prakṛti [[SS 5.61](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.61)], [[SS 6.46](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/6.46)], [[SK 19](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/19)].

Yoga does not formulate release as `advaita` but as `kaivalya`, the cessation of saṃyoga and the isolation of the seer when avidyā falls away [[YS 2.25](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.25)], [[YBh 4.34](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.34)]. Vyāsa glosses this as the `atyantika guṇaviyoga` of puruṣa and as the standing of pure `citiśakti` in its own form, not as merger into a single undifferentiated substance [[YBh 3.50](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.50)], [[YBh 4.34](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.34)]. The closest analogue to non-duality is thus perfected `sattva-puruṣa-anyatā` and the exhaustion of kleśa, after which the puruṣa remains `kevalī` [[YBh 3.55](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.55)].

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika does not admit `advaita` as the erasure of real difference, because `anya` and `ananya` are mutually dependent notions and `ananyatā` cannot be made to abolish `anyatā` [[NBh 2.2.31](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/2.2.31)], [[NBh 2.2.32](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/2.2.32)]. Vātsyāyana accordingly treats a thesis such as `sarvam ekam` as dialectically self-defeating: once everything is collapsed into one, no distinct `sādhanabhūta avayava` remains available for proof [[NBh 4.1.43](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.1.43)]. The school's operative categories are therefore `anyatā`, `pṛthaktva`, and real ontic differentiation among substances and selves, not a final `advaita` [[NBh 2.2.31](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/2.2.31)], [[NBh 4.1.43](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.1.43)].

*Advaya* is the quiescence of *prapañca* disclosed when dependent arising is seen as emptiness and reality is marked as *nirvikalpa* and *anānārtha* [[MMK 24.18](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18)], [[MMK 18.9](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.9)]. Because appropriation through 'I' and 'mine' sustains *upādāna* and birth, non-dual wisdom is inseparable from the cessation of such appropriation [[MMK 18.4](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.4)], [[MMK 18.5](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5)]. Candrakīrti accordingly places *advayabuddhi* alongside *karuṇā* and *bodhicitta* as a constitutive cause of the bodhisattva path [[MAv 1.1](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.1)], [[MAv 1.2](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.2)].
per_school.register_tag: LD+M; reflective reader (śūnya, neither dvaita nor advaita)

per_school.definition: Non-duality is the cessation of *grāhya-grāhaka-dvaya* as cognition becomes established in *vijñapti-mātratā* [[Tr 19](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/19)], [[Tr 26](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/26)]. The dependent flow is overlaid by imagined construction, while the perfected nature is precisely the perpetual absence of that superimposed duality [[Tr 20](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/20)], [[Tr 21](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/21)], [[Tr 25](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/25)]. When object-support is no longer apprehended, cognition turns at the basis into supramundane non-apprehension and the *dharmakāya* [[Tr 28](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/28)], [[Tr 29](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/29)], [[Tr 30](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/30)].

Trika speaks positively of non-duality as `paramādvaita`: `vidyā` is the very form of its realization, and the consummation of practice is the recognition `śivo 'ham advitīyo 'ham` [[ŚSV 2.3.1](cite://ksemaraja/siva-sutra-varttika/2.3.1)], [[ŚSV 3.6.18](cite://ksemaraja/siva-sutra-varttika/3.6.18)]. This `advaita` is not a negation of manifestation, for Abhinavagupta describes all this as one `cinmātratattva` in which the universe stands reflected within autonomous self-luminous consciousness [[TS 2.4](cite://abhinavagupta/tantrasara/2.4)], [[TS 3.1-3.8](cite://abhinavagupta/tantrasara/3.1-3.8)]. Difference is accordingly a mode of self-articulation within one `prakāśa-vimarśa` reality, not a second thing outside Śiva [[TS 3.1-3.8](cite://abhinavagupta/tantrasara/3.1-3.8)], [[ŚSV 2.3.1](cite://ksemaraja/siva-sutra-varttika/2.3.1)].

translator_note: 'Non-duality' is the least bad English gloss, but it still imports modern monism-talk and can suggest a flat denial of plurality. The Sanskrit is more exact: denial of a second independent reality. That denial is then filled out differently by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Vallabha, and Caitanya-line thinkers. Translating *advaita* as 'monism' is especially misleading because it silently imports a Western substance-monism debate and obscures the Indian questions about *bheda*, *upādhi*, *pariṇāma*, and *sublation*.
